title: The Day Everything Almost Broke
description: Two college students, Arjun and Riya, prep for their big hackathon demo. But digital shadows are lurking...

scenes.speaker: Arjun
scenes.dialogue: Riya! You won't believe it. I just got an email. I've been shortlisted for that elite GlobalTech internship!

scenes.speaker: Riya
scenes.dialogue: Wait, seriously? That's amazing! But... let me see that. Didn't they say results would be out next week?

scenes.speaker: Riya
scenes.dialogue: The sender is 'internships@gl0bal-tech.co'. Arjun, take a close look at that address. What should we do?
scenes.question: What's the safest first step with this email?

options.text: Click 'Verify Account' immediately to protect your status.
options.feedback: Too risky! Artificial urgency is a classic phishing tactic.

options.text: Forward the email to the IT department for analysis.
options.feedback: While helpful, you should first check the sender's domain yourself.

options.text: Analyze the sender's domain (globaltech-internships.co) and compare it to the official site.
options.feedback: Excellent! 'globaltech-internships.co' is a sophisticated typo-squatting domain.

options.text: Reply to the email asking for more details about the role.
options.feedback: Engaging confirms your email is active. Never reply to unverified senders.

scenes.speaker: Arjun
scenes.dialogue: Whoa, good catch. Close one. Let's head to the cafe to finish our actual project demo. We need some fresh air.

scenes.speaker: Riya
scenes.dialogue: The cafe WiFi is acting up. There's a new network called 'Cafe_Free_Secure'. It's asking for a 'Security Certificate' installation to connect.
scenes.question: The cafe WiFi requires a 'Root Certificate Profile' installation to bypass an error. What should they do?

options.text: Switch to a mobile hotspot or use a cellular data connection.
options.feedback: Perfect. Never install unknown root profiles; they allow attackers to monitor all your encrypted traffic.

options.text: Install the profile; the UI says it's a 'Security Update'.
options.feedback: Critical Error! Attackers often label malicious profiles as 'Security Updates'.

options.text: Connect but only browse websites that have an 'HTTPS' lock.
options.feedback: If you installed the malicious profile, the lock icon can be faked!

options.text: Try connecting using a guest account to isolate the risk.
options.feedback: Isolation won't prevent network-level interception. Stick to trusted hotspots.

scenes.speaker: Arjun
scenes.dialogue: Hotspot it is. Okay, I'm logging into my university portal to push our latest code. I hope I still remember the password...

scenes.speaker: Riya
scenes.dialogue: Arjun... is that the same password you use for your 'BattleArena' game? You know what they say about password reuse.

scenes.speaker: Arjun
scenes.dialogue: Wait! I just got an MFA notification on my phone. 'Unrecognized login attempt in Mumbai'. But we're in Bangalore!
scenes.question: Arjun receives three back-to-back login notifications (MFA Fatigue). What's the protocol?

options.text: Accept the third one so the phone stops vibrating.
options.feedback: That's exactly what the attacker wants. Never yield to MFA fatigue!

options.text: Immediately signal to the platform that 'This isn't me'.
options.feedback: A good start, but you must also rotate your compromised password immediately.

options.text: Ignore it and put the phone in 'Do Not Disturb' mode.
options.feedback: Ignoring it won't stop the attack. You need active password remediation.

options.text: Deny all requests and trigger a password reset for the account immediately.
options.feedback: Exactly! Recurring pings mean they already have your password. Reset it now!

scenes.speaker: Riya
scenes.dialogue: That was way too close. Someone definitely has your credentials. Let's get back to the lab. I feel like something is off today.

scenes.speaker: Arjun
scenes.dialogue: Look, someone left a USB drive on our desk. 'Project_Demo_FINAL_v2'. Maybe it belongs to Vikram from our team?
scenes.question: The USB has a 'CS Dept. Official' label. Is it safe to use on a lab computer?

options.text: Plug it in; lab computers are hardened against viruses.
options.feedback: Hardware-level 'BadUSB' attacks can bypass most software hardening.

options.text: Connect it to a personal laptop first to check the files.
options.feedback: Now your personal laptop is infected too! Never risk it.

options.text: Leave it alone. Report the found item to the Security Desk or IT Supervisor.
options.feedback: Correct. Fake labels are a common way to bait users into plugging in malicious devices.

options.text: Format the drive using a 'Disk Utility' before opening any files.
options.feedback: The infection can happen the millisecond the hardware is recognized, before you can format.

scenes.speaker: Riya
scenes.dialogue: Definitely don't touch that. I'll message Vikram on the official channel... wait, Arjun, look at your phone. You got a DM on SocialNet.

scenes.speaker: Arjun
scenes.dialogue: It's from a 'GlobalTech Recruiter'. They say they need a 6-digit code sent to my SMS to 'confirm my identity' for the internship. It just arrived!
scenes.question: The 'Recruiter' asks for a code to 'Sync Security Clearances'. How should Arjun respond?

options.text: Terminate contact immediately. Official recruiters never ask for codes via Social DMs.
options.feedback: YES. Sharing that code would grant them access to your account or bypass your MFA.

options.text: Provide the code only after they verify their employee ID.
options.feedback: Employee IDs can be easily faked or stolen. Never share OTPs.

options.text: Send the code so your internship status is synchronized.
options.feedback: This is a direct phishing attack to hijack your secure session!

options.text: Ask them to send the request via an encrypted email instead.
options.feedback: Still risky. Just terminate all contact with suspicious accounts.

scenes.speaker: Riya
scenes.dialogue: Arjun, stop! That's a classic social engineering scam. This whole day has been one giant 'Security 101' test we didn't sign up for.

scenes.speaker: Arjun
scenes.dialogue: You're right. If I hadn't been careful (and if you hadn't nagged me), our project, my accounts, everything... it could have been gone.

scenes.speaker: Riya
scenes.dialogue: Let's finish this demo on our own terms. We don't need shortcuts to succeed—we just need to be smart and secure. Ready?
